questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.section: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
questions.question: What is the laboratory method to prepare ethene (\(\mathrm{C_2H_4}\)) from ethanol (\(\mathrm{C_2H_5OH}\)) using concentrated sulfuric acid?
questions.hint: Water is removed from the alcohol.
questions.explanation: Dehydration of ethanol removes a water molecule to form ethene. Option D, hydration, adds water to an alkene (the reverse reaction).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
source_trace.content_anchor: Dehydration of alcohols with concentrated sulphuric acid

explanations_tiered.definition: Dehydration is the elimination of water from a molecule, often used to convert alcohols to alkenes.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Concentrated \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4}\) acts as a dehydrating agent, removing \(\mathrm{H_2O}\) from ethanol to produce ethene.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Dehydration means 'taking away water', leaving a double bond behind.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The reaction is \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2OH \rightarrow CH_2=CH_2 + H_2O}\). The acid catalyst protonates the OH group, leading to elimination.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Dehydration follows an E1 or E2 mechanism depending on the alcohol structure. For primary alcohols like ethanol, an E2 mechanism is favored under hot concentrated acid conditions.
reasoning_steps: Recognize that the product has one less H2O than the reactant.
Recall the term for removing water (dehydration).
misconceptions: Confusing dehydration (removing water) with hydration (adding water).

glossary_entry.definition: A chemical reaction that removes water from a molecule.

flashcard.front: How is ethene prepared from ethanol in the lab?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Lab prep of ethene from ethanol?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Elimination of water to form an alkene.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Produce ethylene in lab by dehydration

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.section: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
questions.question: In the laboratory preparation of ethene, why is the temperature maintained at approximately \(170^\circ \text{C}\) rather than lower?

options.A: To prevent the formation of diethyl ether
options.C: To slow down the reaction rate

questions.hint: At lower temperatures, a different product forms from two alcohol molecules.
questions.explanation: At \(140^\circ \text{C}\), ethanol undergoes condensation to form diethyl ether. At \(170^\circ \text{C}\), dehydration to ethene is favored. Option B is incorrect; dissolution occurs at room temperature.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
source_trace.content_anchor: heat carefully until the temperature reaches about 170°C

explanations_tiered.definition: Temperature control in ethanol dehydration determines whether alkene or ether forms.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Lower temperatures (140°C) favor intermolecular dehydration to diethyl ether, while higher temperatures (170°C) favor intramolecular dehydration to ethene.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Heat gently, you get ether. Heat strongly, you get ethene (the double bond).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Two competing reactions: 2 \(\mathrm{C_2H_5OH} \rightarrow \mathrm{C_2H_5OC_2H_5} + H_2O\) (ether) at 140°C; \(\mathrm{C_2H_5OH} \rightarrow \mathrm{C_2H_4} + H_2O\) (alkene) at 170°C.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The mechanism involves protonation of OH. At lower T, an SN2 attack by another alcohol yields ether. At higher T, E1/E2 elimination yields the alkene.
reasoning_steps: Recall the two possible products from acid-catalyzed heating of ethanol.
Identify which product forms at lower temperature (ether).
Conclude that higher temperature suppresses ether formation to favor alkene.
misconceptions: Assuming only one product is possible regardless of temperature.

glossary_entry.definition: Temperature control distinguishes between alkene formation (high T) and ether formation (low T).

flashcard.front: Why is 170°C used for ethene preparation from ethanol?
flashcard.back: To prevent ether formation (which occurs at 140°C) and favor elimination.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What product forms at 170°C vs 140°C from ethanol + H2SO4?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Different products favored by different temperatures.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain reaction conditions for alkene preparation
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Lower T favors ether.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.section: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
questions.question: What is the name of the rule that predicts the product when an asymmetric alkene reacts with hydrogen halide (HX)?
questions.hint: The rule states: 'The rich get richer' regarding hydrogen addition.
questions.explanation: Markovnikov's rule states that the hydrogen atom adds to the carbon with more hydrogens, and the halogen adds to the carbon with fewer hydrogens. Option A is for electron configuration, B for equilibrium.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons

explanations_tiered.definition: Markovnikov's rule predicts the regiochemistry of electrophilic addition of HX to unsymmetrical alkenes.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The hydrogen atom attaches to the alkene carbon that already has more hydrogen atoms (the 'rich get richer').
explanations_tiered.beginner: Hydrogen goes to the carbon that already has more hydrogens.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For propene (\(\mathrm{CH_3CH=CH_2}\)), H adds to \(\mathrm{CH_2}\) (2 H's) and Br adds to \(\mathrm{CH}\) (1 H), giving 2-bromopropane.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The rule is explained by carbocation stability. The proton adds to the less substituted carbon to form the more stable secondary/tertiary carbocation intermediate.
reasoning_steps: Identify the rule for addition to unsymmetrical alkenes.
Recall the 'rich get richer' phrase.
Select Markovnikov's rule.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Markovnikov: H adds to C with more H's.

glossary_entry.definition: In addition of HX to an alkene, the hydrogen binds to the carbon with more hydrogens.

flashcard.front: What rule predicts addition of HX to alkenes?
flashcard.back: Markovnikov's rule (H adds to C with more H's).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Markovnikov's rule predicts what for HX addition?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: H adds to the carbon with more hydrogens.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A rule for predicting the major product of HX addition to unsymmetrical alkenes.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Markovnikov's rule predicts regiochemistry.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.section: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
questions.question: What is the main product when propene (\(\mathrm{CH_3CH=CH_2}\)) reacts with hydrogen bromide (\(\mathrm{HBr}\)) according to Markovnikov's rule?
questions.hint: The hydrogen adds to the terminal carbon (\(\mathrm{CH_2}\)) because it has more hydrogens.
questions.explanation: Markovnikov's rule: H adds to C1 (\(\mathrm{CH_2}\), 2 H's), Br adds to C2 (\(\mathrm{CH}\), 1 H), yielding 2-bromopropane. Option A is the anti-Markovnikov product.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons

explanations_tiered.definition: 2-Bromopropane is the alkyl halide formed by Markovnikov addition of HBr to propene.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The double bond in propene is between C1 and C2. C1 has two H's, C2 has one H, so H adds to C1, Br to C2.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The H goes to the end carbon (C1), Br goes to the middle carbon (C2).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Reaction: \(\mathrm{CH_3CH=CH_2 + HBr \rightarrow CH_3CHBrCH_3}\). The secondary carbocation intermediate is more stable than primary.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The mechanism involves protonation of the alkene to form the more stable secondary carbocation, followed by bromide attack at the carbocation center.
reasoning_steps: Identify the two carbons of the double bond.
Count H atoms on each (C1: 2 H, C2: 1 H).
Apply Markovnikov: H to C1, Br to C2.

glossary_entry.definition: The product of Markovnikov addition of HBr to propene.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The product where the hydrogen adds to the less substituted carbon of the alkene.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.section: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
questions.question: Why do alkenes undergo addition reactions readily while alkanes undergo substitution reactions instead?

options.A: Alkenes have a \(\pi\) bond that is easily broken
options.B: Alkenes have stronger C-C bonds than alkanes
options.C: Alkenes are more stable than alkanes

questions.hint: The double bond contains a weak, exposed \(\pi\) bond.
questions.explanation: The \(\pi\) bond in alkenes is weaker and more accessible than the sigma bond, making it susceptible to attack by electrophiles. Alkanes lack \(\pi\) bonds, so they undergo radical substitution.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
source_trace.content_anchor: more reactive than alkanes because the weak bond can be used

explanations_tiered.definition: Addition reactions involve the breaking of a \(\pi\) bond to form two sigma bonds.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The \(\pi\) bond in the carbon-carbon double bond is relatively weak and electron-rich, making it a target for electrophilic addition. Alkanes have no \(\pi\) bonds.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Alkenes have a 'weak spot' (double bond) that wants to grab new atoms.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The \(\pi\) bond has a bond energy of ~260 kJ/mol, while the \(\sigma\) bond is ~350 kJ/mol. The \(\pi\) bond is more easily broken to form two new sigma bonds in addition reactions.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Electrophilic addition proceeds via a \(\pi\) complex and cyclic bromonium ion (for Br2). Saturated alkanes lack the electron density for such electrophilic attack, requiring harsh radical conditions.
reasoning_steps: Recall the structure of alkenes (double bond = sigma + pi).
Recall the structure of alkanes (only sigma bonds).
Compare the reactivity based on the presence of the pi bond.
misconceptions: Thinking alkenes are less reactive because they are 'unsaturated'.

glossary_entry.definition: A covalent bond formed by sideways overlap of p-orbitals, present in double and triple bonds.

flashcard.back: They have a weak \(\pi\) bond that can break to form new sigma bonds.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Alkene has \(\pi\) bond (addition), alkane has only sigma (substitution).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The pi bond is an electron-rich region that attracts electrophiles.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. The \(\pi\) bond is weaker and reactive.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.section: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
questions.question: What is the product when ethene reacts with bromine (\(\mathrm{Br_2}\)) in an inert solvent like carbon tetrachloride?
questions.hint: Bromine adds across the double bond, placing one Br on each carbon.
questions.explanation: The addition reaction gives a dihaloalkane: \(\mathrm{C_2H_4 + Br_2 \rightarrow CH_2BrCH_2Br}\). Option A would require substitution (H replaced by Br), which does not occur under these conditions.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons

explanations_tiered.definition: Halogenation of alkenes is an addition reaction producing a vicinal dihalide.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Bromine adds to the double bond, breaking the \(\pi\) bond and forming two new C-Br sigma bonds, giving 1,2-dibromoethane.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Each carbon in the double bond grabs one bromine atom.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The reaction is \(\mathrm{CH_2=CH_2 + Br_2 \rightarrow CH_2Br-CH_2Br}\). The reddish-brown color of bromine disappears as it is consumed.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The mechanism involves a cyclic bromonium ion intermediate, leading to anti addition. This test is characteristic for alkenes.
reasoning_steps: Identify the type of reaction (addition).
Add one Br to each carbon of the double bond.
Write the product with Br on adjacent carbons.

glossary_entry.definition: A compound with halogen atoms on adjacent carbon atoms.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Addition of halogen across a double bond.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.section: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
questions.question: Polyethylene, a common plastic, is produced from ethene. What type of reaction is involved in this process?
questions.hint: Many small molecules (monomers) join together via their double bonds.
questions.explanation: Addition polymerization involves the joining of alkene monomers without the loss of any small molecule. Ethene monomers add to each other to form long chains. Option B (condensation) loses water or alcohol.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons

explanations_tiered.definition: Addition polymerization is the joining of monomers containing double bonds to form a polymer without byproducts.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The \(\pi\) bond of ethene opens up to link with other ethene molecules, forming a long chain of \(-\mathrm{CH_2-CH_2}-\) units.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Millions of ethene molecules hold hands to make a long plastic chain.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The reaction is \(n\mathrm{CH_2=CH_2} \rightarrow [-\mathrm{CH_2-CH_2}-]_n\). No small molecule is lost; the polymer is the only product.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This is a free radical polymerization. The initiator generates radicals that attack the double bond, propagating the chain.
reasoning_steps: Recall that ethene has a C=C double bond.
Recognize that polymers form from monomers without losing atoms.
Identify as addition polymerization.

glossary_entry.definition: A reaction where monomers add to each other without losing small molecules.

flashcard.front: Polyethylene is made from ethene. What reaction type?

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The process of linking monomers to form a polymer.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Addition polymerization uses the double bond.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.section: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
questions.question: A hydrocarbon decolorizes bromine water and forms a dibromo compound. If 0.1 mol of this hydrocarbon reacts completely with 0.1 mol of \(\mathrm{Br_2}\), what is the number of double bonds per molecule?
questions.hint: One mole of \(\mathrm{Br_2}\) reacts with one mole of alkene for each double bond.
questions.explanation: The 1:1 molar ratio indicates one mole of \(\mathrm{Br_2}\) adds to one mole of hydrocarbon, characteristic of one double bond (mono-alkene). Option A (0) would show no reaction with bromine under dark conditions.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons

explanations_tiered.definition: The stoichiometry of \(\mathrm{Br_2}\) addition indicates the degree of unsaturation.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: A 1:1 molar ratio implies one double bond per molecule because each double bond consumes one mole of \(\mathrm{Br_2}\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: If one molecule takes one Br2 molecule, it has one double bond.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Reaction: \(\mathrm{C=C + Br_2 \rightarrow BrC-CBr}\). 1 mole alkene reacts with 1 mole Br2.
explanations_tiered.advanced: For a triene (3 double bonds), 1 mole would consume 3 moles Br2. The given ratio (0.1:0.1) strictly indicates a mono-ene.
worked_solution: Mole ratio = 0.1 mol HC / 0.1 mol Br2 = 1:1.
reasoning_steps: Compare the moles of hydrocarbon to moles of Br2.
Note that 0.1 mol / 0.1 mol = 1.
Interpret the result as 1 double bond per molecule.
misconceptions: Assuming any reaction implies 2 or 3 double bonds regardless of ratio.

glossary_entry.definition: The number of rings and/or pi bonds in a molecule.

flashcard.front: 0.1 mol HC reacts with 0.1 mol Br2. How many double bonds?

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. 1:1 ratio indicates one double bond.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Mole ratio 1:1 with Br2 means what?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Quantitative test for number of double bonds.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. 1:1 ratio equals one double bond.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.section: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
questions.question: What happens when alkaline \(\mathrm{KMnO_4}\) (Baeyer's reagent) is added to an alkene such as ethene?

options.A: The purple color remains, showing no reaction
options.B: The purple color fades and a brown precipitate (\(\mathrm{MnO_2}\)) forms

questions.explanation: Cold alkaline \(\mathrm{KMnO_4}\) oxidizes alkenes to diols, reducing \(\mathrm{MnO_4^-}\) (purple) to \(\mathrm{MnO_2}\) (brown). Option A describes alkanes, which do not react.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
source_trace.content_anchor: Oxidation of alkenes with cold alkaline potassium permanganate solution

explanations_tiered.definition: Baeyer's reagent (cold dilute \(\mathrm{KMnO_4}\)) tests for unsaturation by forming a diol and \(\mathrm{MnO_2}\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The purple permanganate ion is reduced to brown manganese dioxide while the alkene is oxidized to a diol.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The purple chemical turns brown when it reacts with the double bond.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The permanganate cleaves or dihydroxylates the double bond via a cyclic manganate ester intermediate, resulting in a color change from purple (\(Mn^{VII}\)) to brown (\(Mn^{IV}\)).
reasoning_steps: Recall the appearance of KMnO4 (purple).
Recall the product of oxidation (brown MnO2).
Match to the correct observation.
misconceptions: Thinking the purple color intensifies or stays.

glossary_entry.definition: Cold dilute alkaline potassium permanganate used to test for unsaturation.

flashcard.front: What is the color change in Baeyer's test for alkenes?

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Purple to brown precipitate.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Baeyer's reagent color change for alkene?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Cold alkaline KMnO4, used to test for double bonds.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Distinguish from bromine test (brown to colorless).
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Permanganate is decolorized with MnO2 precipitate.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.section: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a major industrial use of ethene?
questions.hint: Concrete is made from cement, sand, and water.
questions.explanation: Ethene is used to make plastics (polyethylene), alcohols (ethanol), and ripening agents. Option D is incorrect; concrete is an inorganic construction material not derived from ethene.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
source_trace.content_anchor: used as a starting material for the synthesis of many industrial compounds

explanations_tiered.definition: Ethene is a versatile petrochemical feedstock used in polymers, solvents, and agriculture.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Ethene is the precursor for polyethylene, ethanol (by hydration), and ethylene oxide, but not for inorganic materials like concrete.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Industrial uses: \(\mathrm{C_2H_4}\) → (-CH2-CH2-)n (polyethylene), \(\mathrm{C_2H_4 + H_2O \rightarrow C_2H_5OH}\) (ethanol), and fruit ripening. Concrete is not an organic chemical.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Ethene is produced via steam cracking of hydrocarbons. Its largest volume derivative is polyethylene, followed by ethylene oxide, ethylene dichloride, and ethylbenzene.
reasoning_steps: List the known uses of ethene (plastic, alcohol, ripening).
Identify the option unrelated to ethene or organic chemistry.
misconceptions: Assuming all construction materials are organic.

glossary_entry.definition: Ethene is used in the production of polyethylene, ethanol, antifreeze, and as a ripening agent.

flashcard.front: What is not a use of ethene?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which is not a use of ethene?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Raw material supplied to an industrial process.
